summary:
The execution of Louis XVI (January 21, 1793 AD) by guillotine on the Place de la Révolution in Paris was a watershed moment in the [[event:107|French Revolution]] — an act that simultaneously embodied the Revolution's radical logic (that sovereignty resided in the people, not the king, and that the king was a citizen subject to law like any other) and accelerated the Revolution's descent into the [[event:2354|Reign of Terror]]. Louis XVI had been arrested after the storming of the Tuileries palace in August 1792, tried before the National Convention on charges of conspiracy against liberty and crimes against the state, convicted by a nearly unanimous vote, and executed by a bare majority against reprieve. His last words, spoken from the scaffold before the drumbeat drowned him out, reportedly included: 'I die innocent of all the crimes laid to my charge; I pardon those who have occasioned my death.'

The regicide's international impact was immediate and immense. The monarchies of Europe, already alarmed by the Revolution, were now horrified by the deliberate murder of an anointed king — a direct challenge to the principle of divine-right monarchy everywhere. Britain, Spain, and other powers that had been neutral entered the war against France, forming the First Coalition. Internally, the execution polarized France: for the Jacobins and radical republicans it was a necessary assertion of popular sovereignty; for many royalists, moderate republicans, and devout Catholics it was an atrocity that demanded revenge. Marie Antoinette was executed nine months later in October 1793. The Revolution had crossed a threshold from which there was no return, and the path from regicide to the Terror was both logical and short.
